source_name: Asoka, the Buddhist Emperor of India, by Vincent A. Smith (1901)
revision_note: Newly added with 8 verified chunks under public domain (2026-07-23).

content.text: Ashoka inherited the Maurya Empire from his father Bindusara, ruling a vast territory with iron authority before his transformation and dedication to the path of Dharma.
content.source_name: Asoka, the Buddhist Emperor of India, by Vincent A. Smith (1901)

content.text: Directly after the annexation of Kalinga, His Sacred Majesty felt remorse; for the conquest of an unconquered country involves slaughter, death, and captivity of the people, leading Ashoka to embrace Buddhism.
content.source_name: Asoka, the Buddhist Emperor of India, by Vincent A. Smith (1901)

content.text: Ashoka renounced armed conquest (Digvijaya), replacing it with conquest by Dharma (Dharmavijaya), and dedicating imperial resources to public welfare, hospitals, and animal protection.
content.source_name: Asoka, the Buddhist Emperor of India, by Vincent A. Smith (1901)

content.text: Ashoka sent his son Mahendra and daughter Sanghamitra to Sri Lanka as Buddhist monastics to spread the message of peace, sacrificing their companionship for the growth of the Sangha.
content.source_name: Asoka, the Buddhist Emperor of India, by Vincent A. Smith (1901)

content.text: The Emperor undertook long pilgrimages to holy sites like Lumbini and Bodh Gaya, replacing royal hunts with tours of piety, and living a disciplined, simple life dedicated to moral uplift.
content.source_name: Asoka, the Buddhist Emperor of India, by Vincent A. Smith (1901)

content.text: "A man must not do reverence to his own sect by disparaging that of another... concord is meritorious, that they should hear and respect each other's Dharma." (Rock Edict XII on tolerance).
content.source_name: Asoka, the Buddhist Emperor of India, by Vincent A. Smith (1901)

content.text: Ashoka's edicts inscribed on pillars and rocks across Asia established a legacy of state-sponsored compassion, religious harmony, and ethical governance that influenced global history.
content.source_name: Asoka, the Buddhist Emperor of India, by Vincent A. Smith (1901)

content.text: This entry compiles the historical translation of the Rock Edicts from Vincent A. Smith's "Asoka, the Buddhist Emperor of India" (1901), which is in the public domain.
content.source_name: Asoka, the Buddhist Emperor of India, by Vincent A. Smith (1901)
